00032|  Module 2 Transistor:
00033| Introduction
00034| 
00035| 
00036| 
00037| 
00038| 
00039| 
00040| 
00041| 
00042| Beside diodes, the most popular semiconductor devices is transistors.
00043| Transistors are often said to be the most significant invention of the 20th Century.
00044| If cells are the building blocks of life, transistors are the building blocks of the
00045| digital revolution. Without transistors, the technological wonders you use every day
00046| -- cell phones, computers, cars -- would be vastly different, if they existed at all.
00047| Transistors are more complex and can be used in many ways.
00048| Most important feature: can amplify signals and as switch.
00049| Amplification can make weak signal strong (make sounds louder and signal levels
00050| greater), in general, provide function called Gain.
00051| 
00052| Transistor Definition
00053| Transistor is an electronic device made of three layers of semiconductor material that can act as
00054| an insulator and a conductor. The three layered transistor is also known as the bipolar junction
00055| transistor.
00056| Transistor Structure
00057| A transistor has three doped regions.
00058|  For both types, the base is a narrow region sandwiched between the larger collector and
00059| emitter regions.
00060|  The emitter region is heavily doped and its job is to emit carriers into the base.
00061|  The base region is very thin and lightly doped.
00062|  Most of the current carriers injected into the base pass on to the collector.
00063|  The collector region is moderately doped and is the largest of all three regions.
00064|  The junction between emitter and base is called emitter-base junction while the junction
00065| between the collector and base is called collector-base junction.
00066|  As the base is thin and tightly doped, and the emitter is heavily doped, it is wider when
00067| compared to base, the width of the collector is more when compared to both base and
00068| emitter.
00069| 
00070| 
00071| In order to distinguish the emitter and collector an arrow is included in the emitter. The
00072| direction of the arrow depends on the conventional flow of current when emitter base
00073| junction is forward biased.
00074| 
00075|  
00076| 
00077| 
00078| 
00079| 
00080| 
00081| 
00082| Transistor Construction
00083| BJT is bipolar because both holes (+) and electrons (-) will take part in the current flow
00084| through the device
00085| N-type regions contains free electrons (negative carriers)
00086| P-type regions contains free holes (positive carriers)
00087| 2 types of BJT – NPN transistor – PNP transistor
00088| The transistor regions are: –
00089| Emitter (E) – send the carriers into the base region and then on to the collector. The section
00090| of one side that supplies carriers is called emitter. Emitter is always forward biased wr to
00091| 
00092|  
00093| 
00094| 
00095| base so it can supply carrier. For “npn transistor” emitter supply holes to its junction. For
00096| “pnp transistor” emitter supply electrons to its junction.
00097| Base (B) – acts as control region. It can allow none, some or many carriers to flow. The
00098| middle section which forms two pn junction between emitter and collector is called Base.
00099| Collector (C) – collects the carriers. The section on the other side that collects carrier is
00100| called collector. The collector is always reversed biased wrt to base. For “npn transistor”
00101| collector receives holes to its junction. For “pnp transistor” collector receives electrons to
00102| its junction.
00103| 
00104| Figure: NPN Transistor Structure
00105| Operating regions of a transistor
00106| Unbiased Transistor
00107| 
00108| 
00109| 
00110| 
00111| 
00112| 
00113| 
00114| 
00115| When the three terminal of the transistor kept open it is called unbiased transistor.
00116| Since transistor is like two p-n junction diodes connected back to back, there are
00117| depletion region at both the junctions- Emitter junction and Collector junction.
00118| During diffusion process, depletion region penetrates more deeply into the lightly
00119| doped side in order to include an equal number of impurity atoms in the each side of
00120| the junction.
00121| As shown in the figure, depletion region at emitter junction penetrates less in the
00122| heavily doped emitter and extends more in the base region.
00123| Similarly, depletion region at collector junction penetrates less in the heavily doped
00124| collector and extends more in to base region.
00125| As collector is slightly less doped than the emitter, the depletion layer width at the
00126| collector junction is more than the depletion layer width at the emitter junction.
00127| 
00128|  Figure: Unbiased NPN transistor
00129| 
00130| Figure: Unbiased PNP transistor
00131| Transistor biasing
00132| In electronics, 'biasing' usually refers to a fixed DC voltage or current applied to a terminal
00133| of an electronic component such as a diode, transistor or vacuum tube in a circuit in which
00134| AC signals are also present, in order to establish proper operating conditions for the
00135| 
00136|  component. Biasing is the process of providing DC voltage which helps in the functioning
00137| of the circuit.
00138| A transistor can be operated in three different regions as
00139|  Active region
00140|  Saturation region
00141|  Cut-off region
00142| Active region
00143| 
00144| 
00145| 
00146| 
00147| The transistor is said to be operated in active region when the emitter-base junction is
00148| forward biased and collector–base junction is reverse biased.
00149| The collector current is said to have two current components one is due to the forward
00150| biasing of EB junction and the other is due to reverse biasing of CB junction.
00151| 
00152| Saturation region
00153| 
00154| 
00155| 
00156| 
00157| Transistor is said to be operated in saturation region when both EB junction and CB
00158| junction are forward biased as shown in Fig.
00159| When transistor is operated in saturation region Ic increases rapidly for a very small change
00160| in Vc.
00161| 
00162| Cut-off region
00163| 
00164|  
00165| 
00166| 
00167| When both EB junction and CB junction are reverse biased, the transistor is said to be
00168| operated in cutoff region.
00169| The current in the transistor is very small and thus when a transistor in this region it is
00170| assumed to be in off state.
00171| Transistor Working
00172| Working of NPN transistor
00173| 
00174| When external voltage is applied to an npn transistor, it is said to be a biased npn transistor.
00175| Depending on the polarity of the applied voltage, the npn transistor can be operated in three modes:
00176| active mode, cutoff mode and saturation mode.
00177| 
00178| The npn transistor is often operated in active mode because in active mode the npn transistor
00179| amplifies the electric current.
00180| So let’s see how an npn transistor works in active mode.
00181| Let us consider an npn transistor as shown in the below figure. In the below figure, the emitterbase junction is forward biased by the DC voltage VEE and base-collector junction is reverse biased
00182| by the DC voltage VCC.
00183| 
00184|  Emitter-base junction:
00185| Due to the forward bias, a large number of free electrons in the left side n-region (emitter)
00186| experience a repulsive force from the negative terminal of the DC battery and also they experience
00187| an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. When the DC voltage exceeds the knee
00188| voltage, the free electrons start flowing from emitter to base. In the similar way, holes in base
00189| experience a repulsive force from the positive terminal of the battery and also experience an
00190| attractive force from the negative terminal of the battery. As a result, the holes start flowing from
00191| base to emitter.
00192| 
00193|  Thus, the depletion width at the emitter-base junction reduces by applying the forward bias
00194| voltage.
00195| We know that electric current means flow of charge carriers. The free electrons (negative charge
00196| carriers) flow from emitter to base whereas holes (positive charge carriers) flow from base to
00197| emitter. These charge carriers conduct electric current. However, the conventional
00198| current direction is same as the direction of holes.
00199| Thus, the electric current flows from base to emitter.
00200| Base-collector junction:
00201| 
00202| Due to the reverse bias, a large number of free electrons in the right side n-region (collector)
00203| experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. Hence, the free electrons
00204| move away from the junction and flow towards the positive terminal of the battery. As a result, a
00205| large number of neutral collector atoms loses electrons and becomes positive ions. On the other
00206| hand, holes in the p-region (base) experience an attractive force from the negative terminal of the
00207| battery. Hence, the holes move away from the junction and flow towards the negative terminal of
00208| the battery. As a result, a large number of neutral base atoms gains electrons and becomes negative
00209| ions.
00210| Thus, the width of depletion region increases at base-collector junction. In other words, the number
00211| of positive and negative ions increases at the base-collector junction.
00212| 
00213|  Collector-base-emitter current:
00214| 
00215| The free electrons that are flowing from emitter to base due to forward bias will combine with the
00216| holes in the base. However, the base is very thin and lightly doped. So only, a small percentage of
00217| emitter free electrons combines with the holes in the base region. The remaining large number of
00218| free electrons will cross the base region and reaches to the collector region. This is due to the
00219| positive supply voltage applied at collector. Hence, free electrons flow from emitter to collector.
00220| At collector, both the emitter free electrons and collector free electrons produces current by
00221| flowing towards the positive terminal of the battery, so IE = IB + IC. Therefore, an amplified current
00222| is produced at the output.
00223| In npn transistor, the electric current is majorly conducted by free electrons, however the current
00224| conduction in BJT is due to both holes and electrons.
00225| Working of a Biased pnp transistor
00226| When external voltage is applied to a pnp transistor, it is said to be a biased pnp transistor.
00227| Depending on the polarity of the applied voltage, the pnp transistor can be operated in three modes:
00228| active mode, cutoff mode and saturation mode.
00229| 
00230|  The pnp transistor is often operated in active mode because in active mode the pnp transistor
00231| amplifies the electric current.
00232| So let’s see how a pnp transistor works in active mode.
00233| Let us consider a pnp transistor as shown in the below figure. In the below figure, the emitter-base
00234| junction is forward biased by the DC voltage VEE and base-collector junction is reverse biased by
00235| the DC voltage VCC.
00236| Emitter-base junction:
00237| 
00238| Due to the forward bias, a large number of holes in the left side p-region (emitter) experience a
00239| repulsive force from the positive terminal of the DC battery and also they experience an attractive
00240| force from the negative terminal of the battery. When the DC voltage exceeds the knee voltage,
00241| the holes start flowing from emitter to base. In the similar way, free electrons in base experience a
00242| 
00243|  repulsive force from the negative terminal of the battery and also experience an attractive force
00244| from the positive terminal of the battery. As a result, the free electrons start flowing from base to
00245| emitter.
00246| The majority carriers holes carry most of the current from emitter to base. Thus, the electric current
00247| flows from emitter to base.
00248| This electric current flow reduces the width of the depletion region at emitter-base junction.
00249| Base-collector junction:
00250| 
00251| Due to the reverse bias, a large number of holes in the right side n-region (collector) experience
00252| an attractive force from the negative terminal of the battery. Hence, the holes move away from the
00253| junction and flow towards the negative terminal of the battery. As a result, a large number of
00254| neutral collector atoms gains electrons and becomes negative ions. On the other hand, free
00255| electrons in the n-region (base) experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the
00256| battery. Hence, the free electrons move away from the junction and flow towards the positive
00257| terminal of the battery. As a result, a large number of neutral base atoms loses electrons and
00258| becomes positive ions.
00259| Thus, the width of depletion region increases at base-collector junction. In other words, the number
00260| of positive and negative ions increases at the base-collector junction.
00261| 
00262|  Emitter-base-collector current:
00263| 
00264| The holes that are flowing from emitter to base due to forward bias will combines with the free
00265| electrons in the base. However, the base is very thin and lightly doped. So only, a small percentage
00266| of emitter holes will combine with the free electrons in the base region. The remaining large
00267| number of holes will cross the base region and reaches to the collector region. This is due to the
00268| negative supply voltage applied at collector. Hence, the holes flow from emitter to collector. At
00269| collector, both the emitter holes and collector holes produces current by flowing towards the
00270| negative terminal of the battery so, IE = IB + IC. Therefore, an amplified current is produced at the
00271| output.
00272| In pnp transistor, the electric current is majorly conducted by holes, however the current
00273| conduction in BJT is due to both holes and electrons.
00274| Current Relationships and Amplification
00275| Current Amplification Factor (α):
00276| The ratio of collector current to the emitter current is called α of the transistor.
00277|

00279| 
00280|  Base Current Amplification Factor (β):

00360| Problems:
00361| Q1. In a common base connection, IE = 1mA, IC = 0.95mA. Calculate the value of IB .
00362| 
00363| Q2. In a common base connection, current amplification factor is 0.9. If the emitter current
00364| is 1mA, determine the value of base current.
00365| 
00366| Q3. In a common base connection, IC = 0.95 mA and IB = 0.05 mA. Find the value of α.
00367| 
00368| Q4. In a common base connection, the emitter current is 1mA. If the emitter circuit is open,
00369| the collector current is 50 μA. Find the total collector current. Given that α = 0.92.
00370| 
00371|  Q5. In a common base connection, α = 0.95. The voltage drop across 2 kΩ resistance
00372| which is connected in the collector is 2V. Find the base current.
00373| 
00374| Fig. shows the required common base connection.
00375| The voltage drop across RC (= 2 kΩ) is 2V.
00376| 
00377| Transistor Configurations
00378| Common Base (CB) configuration
00379| In common base configuration, emitter is the input terminal, collector is the output terminal and
00380| base terminal is connected as a common terminal for both input and output. That means the emitter
00381| terminal and common base terminal are known as input terminals whereas the collector terminal
00382| and common base terminal are known as output terminals.
00383| In common base configuration, the base terminal is grounded so the common base configuration
00384| is also known as grounded base configuration. Sometimes common base configuration is referred
00385| to as common base amplifier, CB amplifier, or CB configuration.
00386| 
00387|  The input signal is applied between the emitter and base terminals while the corresponding output
00388| signal is taken across the collector and base terminals. Thus the base terminal of a transistor is
00389| common for both input and output terminals and hence it is named as common base configuration.
00390| The supply voltage between base and emitter is denoted by VBE while the supply voltage between
00391| collector and base is denoted by VCB.
00392| As mentioned earlier, in every configuration, the base-emitter junction JE is always forward biased
00393| and collector-base junction JC is always reverse biased. Therefore, in common base configuration,
00394| the base-emitter junction JE is forward biased and collector-base junction JC is reverse biased.
00395| The common base configuration for both NPN and PNP transistors is shown in the below figure.
00396| 
00397|  From the above circuit diagrams of npn and pnp transistors, it can be seen that for both npn and
00398| pnp transistors, the input is applied to the emitter and the output is taken from the collector. The
00399| common terminal for both the circuits is the base.
00400| Current flow in common base amplifier
00401| For the sake of understanding, let us consider NPN transistor in common base configuration.
00402| The npn transistor is formed when a single p-type semiconductor layer is sandwiched between
00403| two n-type semiconductor layers.
00404| 
00405|  The base-emitter junction JE is forward biased by the supply voltage VBE while the collector-base
00406| junction JC is reverse biased by the supply voltage VCB.
00407| Due to the forward bias voltage VBE, the free electrons (majority carriers) in the emitter region
00408| experience a repulsive force from the negative terminal of the battery similarly holes (majority
00409| carriers) in the base region experience a repulsive force from the positive terminal of the battery.
00410| As a result, free electrons start flowing from emitter to base similarly holes start flowing from base
00411| to emitter. Thus free electrons which are flowing from emitter to base and holes which are flowing
00412| from base to emitter conducts electric current. The actual current is carried by free electrons which
00413| are flowing from emitter to base. However, we follow the conventional current direction which is
00414| from base to emitter. Thus electric current is produced at the base and emitter region.
00415| 
00416|  The free electrons which are flowing from emitter to base will combine with the holes in the base
00417| region similarly the holes which are flowing from base to emitter will combine with the electrons
00418| in the emitter region.
00419| From the above figure, it is seen that the width of the base region is very thin. Therefore, only a
00420| small percentage of free electrons from emitter region will combine with the holes in the base
00421| region and the remaining large number of free electrons cross the base region and enters into the
00422| collector region. A large number of free electrons which entered into the collector region will
00423| experience an attractive force from the positive terminal of the battery. Therefore, the free electrons
00424| in the collector region will flow towards the positive terminal of the battery. Thus, electric current
00425| is produced in the collector region.
00426| The electric current produced at the collector region is primarily due to the free electrons from the
00427| emitter region similarly the electric current produced at the base region is also primarily due to the
00428| free electrons from emitter region. Therefore, the emitter current is greater than the base current
00429| and collector current. The emitter current is the sum of base current and collector current.
00430| IE = IB + IC
00431| We know that emitter current is the input current and collector current is the output current.
00432| The output collector current is less than the input emitter current, so the current gain of this
00433| amplifier is actually less than 1. In other words, the common base amplifier attenuates the electric
00434| current rather than amplifying it.
00435| 
00436|  The base-emitter junction JE at input side acts as a forward biased diode. So the common base
00437| amplifier has a low input impedance (low opposition to incoming current). On the other hand, the
00438| collector-base junction JC at output side acts somewhat like a reverse biased diode. So the common
00439| base amplifier has high output impedance.
00440| Therefore, the common base amplifier provides a low input impedance and high output impedance.
00441| Transistors with low input impedance and high output impedance provide a high voltage gain.
00442| Even though the voltage gain is high, the current gain is very low and the overall power gain of
00443| the common base amplifier is low as compared to the other transistor amplifier configurations.
00444| The common base transistor amplifiers are primarily used in the applications where low input
00445| impedance is required.
00446| The common base amplifier is mainly used as a voltage amplifier or current buffer.
00447| This type of transistor arrangement is not very common and is not as widely used as the other two
00448| transistor configurations.
00449| The working principle of pnp transistor with CB configuration is same as the npn transistor with
00450| CB configuration. The only difference is in npn transistor free electrons conduct most of the current
00451| whereas in pnp transistor the holes conduct most of the current.
00452| To fully describe the behavior of a transistor with CB configuration, we need two set of
00453| characteristics: they are
00454| 
00455| 
00456| Input characteristics
00457| 
00458| 
00459| 
00460| Output characteristics.
00461| 
00462| Input characteristics
00463| The input characteristics describe the relationship between input current (IE) and the input voltage
00464| (VBE).
00465| First, draw a vertical line and horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal line
00466| represents x-axis. The input current or emitter current (IE) is taken along the y-axis (vertical line)
00467| and the input voltage (VBE) is taken along the x-axis (horizontal line).
00468| To determine the input characteristics, the output voltage VCB (collector-base voltage) is kept
00469| constant at zero volts and the input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage
00470| levels. For each voltage level of the input voltage (VBE), the input current (IE) is recorded on a
00471| paper or in any other form.
00472| A curve is then drawn between input current IE and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
00473| VCB (0 volts).
00474| 
00475|  Next, the output voltage (VCB) is increased from zero volts to a certain voltage level (8 volts) and
00476| kept constant at 8 volts. While increasing the output voltage (VCB), the input voltage (VBE) is kept
00477| constant at zero volts. After we kept the output voltage (VCB) constant at 8 volts, the input voltage
00478| VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of the input
00479| voltage (VBE), the input current (IE) is recorded on a paper or in any other form.
00480| A curve is then drawn between input current IE and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
00481| VCB (8 volts).
00482| This is repeated for higher fixed values of the output voltage (VCB).
00483| When output voltage (VCB) is at zero volts and emitter-base junction JE is forward biased by the
00484| input voltage (VBE), the emitter-base junction acts like a normal p-n junction diode. So the input
00485| characteristics are same as the forward characteristics of a normal pn junction diode.
00486| The cut in voltage of a silicon transistor is 0.7 volts and germanium transistor is 0.3 volts. In our
00487| case, it is a silicon transistor. So from the above graph, we can see that after 0.7 volts, a small
00488| increase in input voltage (VBE) will rapidly increase the input current (IE).
00489| When the output voltage (VCB) is increased from zero volts to a certain voltage level (8 volts), the
00490| emitter current flow will be increased which in turn reduces the depletion region width at emitterbase junction. As a result, the cut in voltage will be reduced. Therefore, the curves shifted towards
00491| the left side for higher values of output voltage VCB.
00492| 
00493|  Output characteristics
00494| The output characteristics describe the relationship between output current (IC) and the output
00495| voltage (VCB).
00496| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
00497| line represents x-axis. The output current or collector current (IC) is taken along the y-axis (vertical
00498| line) and the output voltage (VCB) is taken along the x-axis (horizontal line).
00499| To determine the output characteristics, the input current or emitter current IE is kept constant at
00500| zero mA and the output voltage VCB is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For
00501| each voltage level of the output voltage VCB, the output current (IC) is recorded.
00502| A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCB at constant input current
00503| IE (0 mA).
00504| When the emitter current or input current IE is equal to 0 mA, the transistor operates in the cut-off
00505| region.
00506| 
00507| Next, the input current (IE) is increased from 0 mA to 1 mA by adjusting the input voltage VBE and
00508| the input current IE is kept constant at 1 mA. While increasing the input current IE, the output
00509| voltage VCB is kept constant.
00510| After we kept the input current (IE) constant at 1 mA, the output voltage (VCB) is increased from
00511| zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of the output voltage (VCB), the output
00512| current (IC) is recorded.
00513| 
00514|  A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCB at constant input current
00515| IE (1 mA). This region is known as the active region of a transistor.
00516| This is repeated for higher fixed values of input current IE (I.e. 2 mA, 3 mA, 4 mA and so on).
00517| From the above characteristics, we can see that for a constant input current IE, when the output
00518| voltage VCB is increased, the output current IC remains constant.
00519| At saturation region, both emitter-base junction JE and collector-base junction JC are forward
00520| biased. From the above graph, we can see that a sudden increase in the collector current when the
00521| output voltage VCB makes the collector-base junction JC forward biased.
00522| Early effect
00523| Due to forward bias, the base-emitter junction JE acts as a forward biased diode and due to reverse
00524| bias, the collector-base junction JC acts as a reverse biased diode.
00525| Therefore, the width of the depletion region at the base-emitter junction JE is very small whereas
00526| the width of the depletion region at the collector-base junction JC is very large.
00527| If the output voltage VCB applied to the collector-base junction JC is further increased, the depletion
00528| region width further increases. The base region is lightly doped as compared to the collector region.
00529| So the depletion region penetrates more into the base region and less into the collector region. As
00530| a result, the width of the base region decreases. This dependency of base width on the output
00531| voltage (VCB) is known as an early effect.
00532| If the output voltage VCB applied to the collector-base junction JC is highly increased, the base
00533| width may be reduced to zero and causes a voltage breakdown in the transistor. This phenomenon
00534| is known as punch through.
00535| 
00536| Common Emitter Configuration
00537| In common emitter configuration, base is the input terminal, collector is the output terminal and
00538| emitter is the common terminal for both input and output. That means the base terminal and
00539| common emitter terminal are known as input terminals whereas collector terminal and common
00540| emitter terminal are known as output terminals.
00541| In common emitter configuration, the emitter terminal is grounded so the common emitter
00542| configuration is also known as grounded emitter configuration. Sometimes common emitter
00543| configuration is also referred to as CE configuration, common emitter amplifier, or CE amplifier.
00544| The common emitter (CE) configuration is the most widely used transistor configuration.
00545| 
00546|  The common emitter (CE) amplifiers are used when large current gain is needed.
00547| The input signal is applied between the base and emitter terminals while the output signal is taken
00548| between the collector and emitter terminals. Thus, the emitter terminal of a transistor is common
00549| for both input and output and hence it is named as common emitter configuration.
00550| The supply voltage between base and emitter is denoted by VBE while the supply voltage between
00551| collector and emitter is denoted by VCE.
00552| In common emitter (CE) configuration, input current or base current is denoted by IB and output
00553| current or collector current is denoted by IC.
00554| The common emitter amplifier has medium input and output impedance levels. So the current gain
00555| and voltage gain of the common emitter amplifier is medium. However, the power gain is high.
00556| To fully describe the behavior of a transistor with CE configuration, we need two set of
00557| characteristics – input characteristics and output characteristics.
00558| Input characteristics
00559| The input characteristics describe the relationship between input current or base current (IB) and
00560| input voltage or base-emitter voltage (VBE).
00561| 
00562|  First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
00563| line represents x-axis. The input current or base current (IB) is taken along y-axis (vertical line)
00564| and the input voltage (VBE) is taken along x-axis (horizontal line).
00565| To determine the input characteristics, the output voltage VCE is kept constant at zero volts and the
00566| input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of
00567| input voltage (VBE), the corresponding input current (IB) is recorded.
00568| 
00569| A curve is then drawn between input current IB and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
00570| VCE (0 volts).
00571| Next, the output voltage (VCE) is increased from zero volts to certain voltage level (10 volts) and
00572| the output voltage (VCE) is kept constant at 10 volts. While increasing the output voltage (VCE),
00573| the input voltage (VBE) is kept constant at zero volts. After we kept the output voltage (VCE)
00574| constant at 10 volts, the input voltage VBE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels.
00575| For each voltage level of input voltage (VBE), the corresponding input current (IB) is recorded.
00576| A curve is then drawn between input current IB and input voltage VBE at constant output voltage
00577| VCE (10 volts).
00578| This process is repeated for higher fixed values of output voltage (VCE).
00579| When output voltage (VCE) is at zero volts and emitter-base junction is forward biased by input
00580| voltage (VBE), the emitter-base junction acts like a normal p-n junction diode. So the input
00581| characteristics of the CE configuration is same as the characteristics of a normal pn junction diode.
00582| The cut in voltage of a silicon transistor is 0.7 volts and germanium transistor is 0.3 volts. In our
00583| case, it is a silicon transistor. So from the above graph, we can see that after 0.7 volts, a small
00584| increase in input voltage (VBE) will rapidly increases the input current (IB).
00585| 
00586|  In common emitter (CE) configuration, the input current (IB) is very small as compared to the input
00587| current (IE) in common base (CB) configuration. The input current in CE configuration is
00588| measured in microamperes (μA) whereas the input current in CB configuration is measured in
00589| milliamperes (mA).
00590| In common emitter (CE) configuration, the input current (IB) is produced in the base region which
00591| is lightly doped and has small width. So the base region produces only a small input current (IB).
00592| On the other hand, in common base (CB) configuration, the input current (IE) is produced in the
00593| emitter region which is heavily doped and has large width. So the emitter region produces a large
00594| input current (IE). Therefore, the input current (IB) produced in the common emitter (CE)
00595| configuration is small as compared to the common base (CB) configuration.
00596| Due to forward bias, the emitter-base junction acts as a forward biased diode and due to reverse
00597| bias, the collector-base junction acts as a reverse biased diode.
00598| Therefore, the width of the depletion region at the emitter-base junction is very small whereas the
00599| width of the depletion region at the collector-base junction is very large.
00600| If the output voltage VCE applied to the collector-base junction is further increased, the depletion
00601| region width further increases. The base region is lightly doped as compared to the collector region.
00602| So the depletion region penetrates more into the base region and less into the collector region. As
00603| a result, the width of the base region decreases which in turn reduces the input current (IB) produced
00604| in the base region.
00605| From the above characteristics, we can see that for higher fixed values of output voltage VCE, the
00606| curve shifts to the right side. This is because for higher fixed values of output voltage, the cut in
00607| voltage is increased above 0.7 volts. Therefore, to overcome this cut in voltage, more input voltage
00608| VBE is needed than previous case.
00609| Output characteristics
00610| The output characteristics describe the relationship between output current (IC) and output voltage
00611| (VCE).
00612| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
00613| line represents x-axis. The output current or collector current (IC) is taken along y-axis (vertical
00614| line) and the output voltage (VCE) is taken along x-axis (horizontal line).
00615| To determine the output characteristics, the input current or base current IB is kept constant at
00616| 0 μA and the output voltage VCE is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each
00617| level of output voltage, the corresponding output current (IC) is recorded.
00618| 
00619|  A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCE at constant input current
00620| IB (0 μA).
00621| When the base current or input current IB = 0 μA, the transistor operates in the cut-off region. In
00622| this region, both junctions are reverse biased.
00623| Next, the input current (IB) is increased from 0 μA to 20 μA by adjusting the input voltage (VBE).
00624| The input current (IB) is kept constant at 20 μA.
00625| While increasing the input current (IB), the output voltage (VCE) is kept constant at 0 volts.
00626| After we kept the input current (IB) constant at 20 μA, the output voltage (VCE) is increased from
00627| zero volts to different voltage levels. For each voltage level of output voltage (VCE), the
00628| corresponding output current (IC) is recorded.
00629| A curve is then drawn between output current IC and output voltage VCE at constant input current
00630| IB (20 μA). This region is known as the active region of a transistor. In this region, emitter-base
00631| junction is forward biased and the collector-base junction is reverse biased.
00632| This steps are repeated for higher fixed values of input current IB (I.e. 40 μA, 60 μA, 80 μA and
00633| so on).
00634| When output voltage VCE is reduced to a small value (0.2 V), the collector-base junction becomes
00635| forward biased. This is because the output voltage VCE has less effect on collector-base junction
00636| than input voltage VBE.
00637| 
00638|  As we know that the emitter-base junction is already forward biased. Therefore, when both the
00639| junctions are forward biased, the transistor operates in the saturation region. In this region, a small
00640| increase in output voltage VCE will rapidly increases the output current IC.
00641| 
00642| Common Collector Configuration
00643| In this configuration, the base terminal of the transistor serves as the input, the emitter terminal is
00644| the output and the collector terminal is common for both input and output. Hence, it is named as
00645| common collector configuration. The input is applied between the base and collector while the
00646| output is taken from the emitter and collector.
00647| In common collector configuration, the collector terminal is grounded so the common collector
00648| configuration is also known as grounded collector configuration.
00649| 
00650|  Sometimes common collector configuration is also referred to as emitter follower, voltage
00651| follower, common collector amplifier, CC amplifier, or CC configuration. This configuration is
00652| mostly used as a voltage buffer.
00653| The input supply voltage between base and collector is denoted by VBC while the output voltage
00654| between emitter and collector is denoted by VEC.
00655| In this configuration, input current or base current is denoted by IB and output current or emitter
00656| current is denoted by IE.The common collector amplifier has high input impedance and low output
00657| impedance. It has low voltage gain and high current gain.
00658| The power gain of the common collector amplifier is medium. To fully describe the behavior of a
00659| transistor with CC configuration, we need two set of characteristics - input characteristics and
00660| output characteristics.
00661| Input characteristics
00662| The input characteristics describe the relationship between input current or base current (IB) and
00663| input voltage or base-collector voltage (VBC).
00664| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
00665| line represents x-ax
00666| The input current or base current (IB) is taken along y-axis (vertical line) and the input voltage or
00667| base-collector voltage (VBC) is taken along x-axis (horizontal line).
00668| To determine the input characteristics, the output voltage VEC is kept constant at 3V and the input
00669| voltage VBC is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each level of input
00670| voltage VBC, the corresponding input current IB is noted. A curve is then drawn between input
00671| current IB and input voltage VBC at constant output voltage VEC (3V).
00672| 
00673| Next, the output voltage VEC is increased from 3V to different voltage level, say for example 5V
00674| and then kept constant at 5V. While increasing the output voltage VEC, the input voltage VBC is
00675| 
00676|  kept constant at zero volts.
00677| 
00678| After we kept the output voltage VEC constant at 5V, the input voltage VBC is increased from zero
00679| volts to different voltage levels. For each level of input voltage VBC, the corresponding input
00680| current IB is noted. A curve is then drawn between input current IB and input voltage VBC at constant
00681| output voltage VEC (5V).
00682| This process is repeated for higher fixed values of output voltage (VEC).
00683| Output characteristics
00684| The output characteristics describe the relationship between output current or emitter current (IE)
00685| and output voltage or emitter-collector voltage (VEC).
00686| First, draw a vertical line and a horizontal line. The vertical line represents y-axis and horizontal
00687| line represents x-axis.The output current or emitter current (IE) is taken along y-axis (vertical line)
00688| and the output voltage or emitter-collector
00689| voltage (VEC) is
00690| taken along x-axis
00691| (horizontal line).
00692| To determine the output characteristics, the input current IB is kept constant at zero micro amperes
00693| and the output voltage VEC is increased from zero volts to different voltage levels. For each level
00694| of output voltage VEC, the corresponding output current IE is noted. A curve is then drawn between
00695| output current IE and output voltage VEC at constant input current IB (0 μA).
00696| 
00697| Next, the input current (IB) is increased from 0 μA to 20 μA and then kept constant at 20 μA. While
00698| increasing the input current (IB), the output voltage (VEC) is kept constant at 0 volts.
00699| After we kept the input current (IB) constant at 20 μA, the output voltage (VEC) is increased from
00700| zero volts to different voltage levels. For each level of output voltage (VEC), the corresponding
00701| 
00702|  output current (IE) is recorded. A curve is then drawn between output current IE and
00703| output voltage VEC at constant input current IB (20μA). This region is known as the
00704| active region of a transistor. This process is repeated for higher fixed values of input
00705| current IB (I.e. 40 μA, 60 μA, 80 μA and so on).
00706| In common collector configuration, if the input current or base current is zero then the
00707| output current or emitter current is also zero. As a result, no current flows through the
00708| transistor. So the transistor will be in the cutoff region. If the base current is slightly
00709| increased then the output current or emitter current also increases. So the transistor falls
00710| into the active region. If the base current is heavily increased then the current flowing
00711| through the transistor also heavily increases. As a result, the transistor falls into the
00712| saturation region.
00713| 
00714|  2. FIELDF EFFECT TRANSISTOR
00715| 2.1 Introduction
00716| 
00717| 
00718| The Field Effect Transistor abbreviated as FET is another semiconductor device like a
00719| BJT which can be used as an amplifier or switch. Like BJT,
00720| 
00721| 
00722| 
00723| FET is also a three terminal device. However, the principle of operation FET is
00724| completely different from that of BJT. The three terminals of FET's are named as:
00725| Drain (D), Source (S) and Gate (G)
00726| 
00727| 
00728| 
00729| BJT is a current controlled device. On the other hand, in FET, the voltage applied
00730| between gate and source (VGs) controls the output current Ip therefore; FET is a
00731| voltage controlled device.
00732| 
00733| 
00734| 
00735| The name "Field Effect" is derived from the fact that the output current flow is
00736| controlled by an electric field set up in the device by an externally applied voltage
00737| between gate and source terminals (VGs).
00738| 
00739| 
00740| 
00741| BJT is called a bipolar device because here, the conduction of current is due to both
00742| electrons and holes (both Majority & Minority charge carriers). However, FET is
00743| called a unipolar device because here, the conduction of current is only due to
00744| majority charge carriers.
00745| 2.2 Types of FET
00746| 
00747| 
00748| 
00749| r
00750| d
00751| b
00752| i
00753| a
00754| s
00755| e JFET Introduction and symbol
00756| 2.3
00757| d  JFET is a unipolar-transistor, which acts as a voltage controlled current device
00758| .
00759| and is a device in which current at two electrodes is controlled by the action of an electric
00760| 
00761| field at a p-n junction.
00762| 
00763| 
00764| A JFET, or junction field-effect transistor, or JUGFET, is a FET in which the gate is
00765| created by reverse-biased junction (as opposed to the MOSFET which creates a junction
00766| via a field generated by conductive gate, separated from the gate region by a thin
00767| insulator).
00768| 
00769|  2.4 Construction
00770| 2.4.1 n-channel JFET
00771| The figure shows construction and symbol of n-channel JFET. A small bar of extrinsic
00772| semiconductor material, n type is taken and its two ends, two ohmic contacts are made which is
00773| the drain and source terminals of FET. Heavily doped electrodes of p type material form p-n
00774| junctions on each side of the bar. The thin region between the two p gates is called the channel.
00775| Since this channel is in the n type bar, the FET is known as n-channel JFET.
00776| 
00777|  The electrons enter the channel through the terminal called source and leave through the terminal
00778| called drain. The terminals taken out from heavily doped electronics of p type material are called
00779| gates. These electrodes are connected together and only one terminal is taken out, which is called
00780| gate, as shown in the figure.
00781| 2.4.2 p-channel JFET
00782| The device could be made of p type bar with two n type gates as shown in the figure. This will be
00783| p-channel JFET. The principle of working of n-channel JFET and p- channel JFET are similar.
00784| The only difference being that in n-channel JFET the current is carried by electrons while in pchannel JFET, it is carried by holes.
00785| 
00786| 2.5 Operation of n-channel JFET
00787|  In JFET, the p-n junction between gate and source is always kept in reverse biased
00788| conditions. Since the current in a reverse biased p-n junction is extremely small,
00789| practically zero. The gate current in JFET is often neglected and assumed to be zero.
00790| 
00791|  
00792| 
00793| Let us consider the circuit in the figure, voltage VDD is applied between drain and source.
00794| Gate terminal is kept open. The bar is of n-type material. Due to the polarities of applied
00795| voltage as shown in the fig, the majority carriers i.e. the electrons start flowing from the
00796| source to the drain. The flow of electrons makes the drain current, I D.
00797| 
00798| 
00799| 
00800| The majority carriers move from source to drain through the space between the gate
00801| regions. The space is commonly known as channel. The width of this channel can be
00802| controlled by varying the gate voltage. To see the effect of gate voltage on channel- width
00803| and on drain current ID, consider the diagram below.
00804| 
00805| 
00806| 
00807| The figure (a) shows that an n-channel JFET with the gate directly connected to the
00808| source terminal. When drain voltage VDS is applied, a drain current ID flows in the
00809| direction shown. Since the n-material is resistive, the drain current causes a voltage drop
00810| along the channel. This voltage drop reverse biases the pn junctions, and causes the
00811| depletion regions to penetrate into the channel. Since gate is heavily doped and the
00812| channel is lightly doped the width of the depletion region will mainly be spread in the
00813| channel as shown in fig (a). This penetration depends on the reverse bias voltage. From
00814| 
00815| the fig it can be observed that depletion region width is more at the drain side as compared to
00816| source side because near the junction, voltage at drain side is more than the voltage at the source
00817| side. This shows that reverse bias is not uniform near the junction as it gradually increases from
00818| 
00819|  source side to drain side.
00820| 
00821| 
00822| The depletion region does not contain charge carriers. The space between two depletion
00823| regions is available for conducting portion of the channel. When reverse bias voltage is
00824| applied externally to the gate, the reverse bias will increase and hence increase the
00825| penetration of the depletion region which reduces the width of the conducting portion of
00826| the channel. When the width of the conducting portion of the channel reduces, the no. of
00827| electrons flowing from source to drain reduces and hence the current flowing from drain
00828| to source reduces.
00829| 
00830|  When the external reverse bias voltage at the gate is increased as shown in fig
00831| (b) & (c) the depletion regions will increase more and at a particular stage the width of the
00832| depletion region will be equal to the original width of the depletion regions will increase more
00833| and more, and stage will come when the width of the depletion regions will be equal to the
00834| original width of the channel, leaving zero width for conducting portion of the channel, as shown
00835| in the fig (c). This will prevent any current flow from drain to source and this will cut off the
00836| drain current. The gate to source voltage that produces cutoff is known as cutoff voltage (V GS
00837| (OFF)).
00838| 
00839| 
00840| 
00841| When the gate is shorted to source, there is minimum reverse bias between gate and
00842| source p-n junction, making depletion region width minimum and conducting channel
00843| width maximum. In this case maximum drain current flows which are designated by I DSS
00844| and this are the possible drain current in JFET. It is clear that the gate to source voltage
00845| controls the current flowing through the channel and hence FET is also called voltage
00846| controlled current source.
00847| 
00848| Drain (or) current voltage characteristics of JFET
00849| The current voltage characteristics of an n-channel JFET is shown in the figure. The drain
00850| current (ID) is plotted with VDS for different values of VGS.
00851| 
00852|  3.MOSFET
00853| 3.1 Introduction
00854|  MOSFET stands for metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistor.
00855|  It is capable of voltage gain and signal power gain.
00856|  The MOSFET is the core of integrated circuit designed as thousands of these can be
00857| fabricated in a single chip because of its very small size.
00858|  Every modern electronic system consists of VLST technology and without MOSFET, large
00859| scale integration is impossible.
00860|  It is a four terminals device. The drain and source terminals are connected to the heavily
00861| doped regions. The gate terminal is connected top on the oxide layer and the substrate or
00862| body terminal is connected to the intrinsic semiconductor
00863| 
00864| 3.2 Enhancement n-channel Mosfet
00865| Enhancement type MOSFETS are normally off which means when an enhancement-type MOSFET
00866| is connected, there will be no flow of current from the terminal drain (D) to the source (S) when no
00867| voltage is given to its gate terminal. This is the reason to call this transistor a normally off device.
00868| 
00869| Similarly, if the voltage is given to the gate terminal of this MOSFET, then the drain-source channel
00870| will become very less resistive. When the voltage from gate to source terminal increases then the
00871| flow of current from drain to source terminal will also increase until the highest current is supplied
00872| from drain terminal to source.
00873| Construction
00874| This MOSFET includes three layers gate, drain, and source. The body of MOSFET is known as a
00875| 
00876|  substrate that is connected internally to the source. In the MOSFET, the metallic gate terminal from
00877| the semiconductor layer is insulated through a silicon dioxide layer otherwise a dielectric layer.
00878| 
00879| This EMOSFET is constructed with two materials like P-type and N-type semiconductors. A
00880| substrate gives physical support to the device. A thin SiO layer and an outstanding electrical
00881| insulator simply cover the region in between the source & drain terminals. On the oxide layer, a
00882| metallic layer forms the gate electrode.
00883| In this construction, the two N regions are separated through some micrometers distance over a
00884| lightly doped p-type substrate. These two N-regions are performed like the source and drain
00885| terminals. On the surface, a thin insulation layer is developed which is known as silicon dioxide. The
00886| charge carriers like holes made on this layer will establish aluminum contacts for both the source &
00887| the drain terminals.
00888| This conduction layer works like the terminal gate which is laid on the SiO2 as well as the complete
00889| area of the channel. However for conduction, it doesn’t contain any physical channel In this kind of
00890| enhancement MOSFET, the p-type substrate is extended on the whole SiO2 layer.
00891| Working
00892| The working of EMOSFET is when VGS is 0V then there is no channel that will connect the source
00893| & drain. The p-type substrate has only a small number of thermally produced minority charge
00894| carriers like free electrons thus the drain current is zero. Because of this reason, this MOSFET will
00895| be normally OFF.
00896| Once the gate (G) is positive (+ve), then it attracts minority charge carriers like electrons from p–
00897| substrate where these charge carriers will combine through the holes under the layer of SiO2. Further
00898| VGS is increased then the electrons will have enough potential to overcome bonding and more
00899| charge carriers i.e. electrons get deposited in the channel.
00900| Here, the dielectric is used to prevent the electron’s movement across the silicon dioxide layer. This
00901| accumulation will result in the n-channel formation between Drain and Source terminals. So this can
00902| lead to the generated drain current flow throughout the channel. This drain current is simply
00903| proportional to the channel’s resistance which depends further on the charge carriers attracted to the
00904| +ve terminal of the gate.
00905| 3.3 Depletion Type n-channel MOSFET
00906| Construction
00907| Figure shows the construction of an N-channel DE-MOSFET. It consists of a lightly doped P-type
00908| substrate into which two blocks of heavily doped N-type material are diffused forming the source
00909| and drain. An N-channel is formed by diffusion between the source and drain. The type of impurity
00910| for the channel is the same as for the source and drain. Now a thin layer of SiO 2 dielectric is grown
00911| over the entire surface and holes are cut through the SiO 2 (silicon dioxide) layer to make contact
00912| 
00913|  with the N-type blocks (Source and Drain). Metal is deposited through the holes to provide drain and
00914| source terminals, and on the surface area between drain and source, a metal plate is deposited
00915| 
00916| working
00917| It is obvious from Fig. that there is no P-N junction between gate and channel. Here the diffused
00918| channel N, the insulating dielectric SiO2 layer and the metal layer of the gate forms a parallel plate
00919| capacitor. DE-MOSFET can be operated with either a positive or a negative gate. When gate is
00920| positive with respect to the source it operates in the enhancement—or E-mode and when the gate is
00921| negative w.r.t. the source, as illustrated in Fig. 13.68, it operates in depletion-mode.
